mn22:1.2: At one time the Bhagavā was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn22:10.1: Here, bhikkhus, some foolish men learn the *dhamma*—
mn22:10.10: Suppose, bhikkhus, a man in need of a snake, searching for a snake, wandering in search of a snake,
mn22:10.11: were to see a large snake.
mn22:10.12: He might seize it by the coils or by the tail.
mn22:10.13: Then that snake would turn back and bite him on the hand, arm, or some other limb.
mn22:10.14: Because of that he might meet death or deadly *dukkha*.
mn22:10.16: Because, bhikkhus, the snake was wrongly grasped.
mn22:10.17: In the same way, bhikkhus, here some foolish men learn the *dhamma*—
mn22:10.18: *sutta*, mixed prose and verse, expositions, verses, inspired utterances, sayings, birth stories, marvels, and catechisms.
mn22:10.19: Having learned that *dhamma*, they do not examine the meaning of those *dhammas* with discernment [*paññāya*].
mn22:10.2: *sutta*, mixed prose and verse, expositions, verses, inspired utterances, sayings, birth stories, marvels, and catechisms.
mn22:10.20: Since the meaning of those *dhammas* has not been examined with discernment [*paññāya*], those *dhammas* do not stand up to examination.
mn22:10.21: They learn the *dhamma* both for the advantage of fault-finding and for the advantage of winning arguments.
mn22:10.22: And they do not experience the meaning for whose sake they learn the *dhamma*.
mn22:10.23: Those *dhammas*, wrongly grasped by them, lead for a long time to harm and *dukkha*.
mn22:10.25: Because, bhikkhus, the *dhammas* were wrongly grasped.
mn22:10.3: Having learned that *dhamma*, they do not examine the meaning of those *dhammas* with discernment [*paññāya*].
mn22:10.4: Since the meaning of those *dhammas* has not been examined with discernment [*paññāya*], those *dhammas* do not stand up to examination.
mn22:10.5: They learn the *dhamma* both for the advantage of fault-finding and for the advantage of winning arguments.
mn22:10.6: And they do not experience the meaning for whose sake they learn the *dhamma*.
mn22:10.7: Those *dhammas*, wrongly grasped by them, lead for a long time to harm and *dukkha*.
mn22:10.9: Because, bhikkhus, the *dhammas* were wrongly grasped.
mn22:11.1: But here, bhikkhus, some clansmen learn the *dhamma*—
mn22:11.10: Suppose, bhikkhus, a man in need of a snake, searching for a snake, wandering in search of a snake,
mn22:11.11: were to see a large snake.
mn22:11.12: He would pin it down well with a forked stick.
mn22:11.13: Having pinned it down well with a forked stick, he would seize it securely by the neck.
mn22:11.14: Although that snake might coil its body around that man’s hand, arm, or some other limb, still he would not because of that meet death or deadly *dukkha*.
mn22:11.16: Because, bhikkhus, the snake was well grasped.
mn22:11.17: In the same way, bhikkhus, here some clansmen learn the *dhamma*—
mn22:11.18: *sutta*, mixed prose and verse, expositions, verses, inspired utterances, sayings, birth stories, marvels, and catechisms.
mn22:11.19: Having learned that *dhamma*, they examine the meaning of those *dhammas* with discernment [*paññāya*].
mn22:11.2: *sutta*, mixed prose and verse, expositions, verses, inspired utterances, sayings, birth stories, marvels, and catechisms.
mn22:11.20: Since the meaning of those *dhammas* has been examined with discernment [*paññāya*], those *dhammas* stand up to examination.
mn22:11.21: They do not learn the *dhamma* either for the advantage of fault-finding or for the advantage of winning arguments.
mn22:11.22: And they experience the meaning for whose sake they learn the *dhamma*.
mn22:11.23: Those *dhammas*, well grasped by them, lead for a long time to benefit, welfare, and *sukha*.
mn22:11.25: Because, bhikkhus, the *dhammas* were well grasped.
mn22:11.3: Having learned that *dhamma*, they examine the meaning of those *dhammas* with discernment [*paññāya*].
mn22:11.4: Since the meaning of those *dhammas* has been examined with discernment [*paññāya*], those *dhammas* stand up to examination.
mn22:11.5: They do not learn the *dhamma* either for the advantage of fault-finding or for the advantage of winning arguments.
mn22:11.6: And they experience the meaning for whose sake they learn the *dhamma*.
mn22:11.7: Those *dhammas*, well grasped by them, lead for a long time to benefit and *sukha*.
mn22:11.9: Because, bhikkhus, the *dhammas* were well grasped.
mn22:12.1: Therefore, bhikkhus, when you understand the meaning of what I have said, you should remember it accordingly.
mn22:12.2: And when you do not understand the meaning of what I have said, you should question me about it, or those *bhikkhus* who are skilled.
mn22:13.1: Bhikkhus, I will teach you the simile of the raft: the *dhamma* is for crossing over, not for grasping.
mn22:13.10: But there is no boat or bridge for going from this side to the far side.
mn22:13.11: Why don’t I gather grass, sticks, branches, and leaves, bind them into a raft, and relying on that raft, striving with my hands and feet, cross safely to the far shore?’
mn22:13.12: Then, bhikkhus, that man, having gathered grass, sticks, branches, and leaves and bound them into a raft, relying on that raft and striving with his hands and feet, would cross safely to the far shore.
mn22:13.13: Having crossed over and reached the far shore, it would occur to him:
mn22:13.14: ‘This raft has been very helpful to me.
mn22:13.15: Relying on this raft and striving with my hands and feet, I have crossed safely to the far shore.
mn22:13.16: Why don’t I lift this raft onto my head or carry it on my shoulder, and go wherever I like?’
mn22:13.18: would that man, by acting that way, be doing what should be done with that raft?”
mn22:13.2: Listen to that, pay appropriate attention [*yoniso manasikarotha*], I will speak.”
mn22:13.20: “And how, bhikkhus, would that man be doing what should be done with that raft?
mn22:13.21: Here, bhikkhus, having crossed over and reached the far shore, it would occur to that man:
mn22:13.22: ‘This raft has been very helpful to me.
mn22:13.23: Relying on this raft and striving with my hands and feet, I have crossed safely to the far shore.
mn22:13.24: Why don’t I haul this raft up on dry land or let it sink in the water, and go wherever I like?’
mn22:13.25: Acting that way, bhikkhus, that man would be doing what should be done with that raft.
mn22:13.26: In the same way, bhikkhus, I have taught the *dhamma* as like a raft, for crossing over, not for grasping.
mn22:13.3: “Yes, bhante,” those *bhikkhus* replied to the Bhagavā.
mn22:13.5: “Suppose, bhikkhus, a man were traveling along a road.
mn22:13.6: He would see a great expanse of water, with this shore dangerous and fearful, and the far shore secure and free from fear;
mn22:13.7: but there would be no boat or bridge for going from this side to the far side.
mn22:13.9: ‘Here is this great expanse of water. This shore is dangerous and fearful, and the far shore secure and free from fear.
mn22:14.1: Bhikkhus, when you understand the *dhamma* as like a raft, even *dhammās* should be abandoned by you, how much more so non-*dhammās*.
mn22:15.1: Bhikkhus, these are the six grounds for views.
mn22:15.10: ‘That is the world, that is the self. After death I shall be permanent, stable, everlasting, not liable to change; I shall remain just so for eternity’—
mn22:15.11: that too he regards thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self.’
mn22:15.3: Here, bhikkhus, an uninstructed ordinary person, who has not seen the noble ones, is unskilled in the noble *dhamma*, undisciplined in the noble *dhamma*, who has not seen good persons, is unskilled in the *dhamma* of good persons, undisciplined in the *dhamma* of good persons,
mn22:15.4: regards *rūpa* thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’;
mn22:15.5: regards *vedanā* thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’;
mn22:15.6: regards *saññā* thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’;
mn22:15.7: regards *saṅkhāras* thus: ‘These are mine, I am this, this is my self’;
mn22:15.8: and whatever is seen, heard, sensed, cognized, attained, sought after, and explored by the mind [*manasā*], that too he regards thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’;
mn22:16.1: But, bhikkhus, an instructed noble disciple, who has seen the noble ones, is skilled in the noble *dhamma*, well disciplined in the noble *dhamma*, who has seen good persons, is skilled in the *dhamma* of good persons, well disciplined in the *dhamma* of good persons,
mn22:16.2: regards *rūpa* thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’;
mn22:16.3: regards *vedanā* thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’;
mn22:16.4: regards *saññā* thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’;
mn22:16.5: regards *saṅkhāras* thus: ‘These are not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’;
mn22:16.6: and whatever is seen, heard, sensed, cognized, attained, sought after, and explored by the mind [*manasā*], that too he regards thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’;
mn22:16.8: ‘That is the world, that is the self. After death I shall be permanent, stable, everlasting, not liable to change; I shall remain just so for eternity’—
mn22:16.9: that too he regards thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn22:17.1: Seeing thus, he does not become distressed over what is absent.”
mn22:18.1: When this was said, a certain *bhikkhu* said this to the Bhagavā:
mn22:18.2: “Bhante, could there be distress over what is external when it is absent?”
mn22:18.5: ‘Oh, I had it; now I do not have it.
mn22:18.6: Oh, I might have it; but I do not obtain it.’
mn22:18.7: He sorrows, wears himself out, laments, beats his breast and weeps, and falls into confusion.
mn22:18.8: That is how, *bhikkhu*, there is distress over what is external when it is absent.”
mn22:19.1: “But, bhante, could there be non-distress over what is external when it is absent?”
mn22:19.3: “Here, *bhikkhu*, it does not occur to someone:
mn22:19.4: ‘Oh, I had it; now I do not have it.
mn22:19.5: Oh, I might have it; but I do not obtain it.’
mn22:19.6: He does not sorrow, does not wear himself out, does not lament, does not beat his breast and weep, and does not fall into confusion.
mn22:19.7: That is how, *bhikkhu*, there is non-distress over what is external when it is absent.”
mn22:2.1: Now at that time, a bad view had arisen in a *bhikkhu* named Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter:
mn22:2.2: “I understand the *dhamma* taught by the Bhagavā like this: those obstructive *dhammas* spoken of by the Bhagavā are not capable of obstructing one who engages in them.”
mn22:2.4: “It seems that a bad view has arisen in the *bhikkhu* named Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter:
mn22:2.5: ‘I understand the *dhamma* taught by the Bhagavā like this: those obstructive *dhammas* spoken of by the Bhagavā are not capable of obstructing one who engages in them.’”
mn22:20.1: “Bhante, could there be distress over what is internal when it is absent?”
mn22:20.4: ‘That is the world, that is the self. After death I shall be permanent, stable, everlasting, not liable to change; I shall remain just so for eternity.’
mn22:20.5: He hears the Tathāgata or a disciple of the Tathāgata teaching the *dhamma* for the uprooting of all the underlying tendencies to the standpoints, fixations, obsessions, and latent clingings of views, for the settling [*sabbasaṅkhārasamathāya*] of all *saṅkhāras*, for the relinquishment of all acquisitions, for the ending of *taṇhā*, for dispassion, cessation, and *nibbāna*.
mn22:20.7: ‘So I shall be annihilated! So I shall perish! So I shall no longer exist!’
mn22:20.8: He sorrows, wears himself out, laments, beats his breast and weeps, and falls into confusion.
mn22:20.9: That is how, *bhikkhu*, there is distress over what is internal when it is absent.”
mn22:21.1: “But, bhante, could there be non-distress over what is internal when it is absent?”
mn22:21.3: “Here, *bhikkhu*, someone does not have this view:
mn22:21.4: ‘That is the world, that is the self. After death I shall be permanent, stable, everlasting, not liable to change; I shall remain just so for eternity.’
mn22:21.5: He hears the Tathāgata or a disciple of the Tathāgata teaching the *dhamma* for the uprooting of all the underlying tendencies to the standpoints, fixations, obsessions, and latent clingings of views, for the settling [*sabbasaṅkhārasamathāya*] of all *saṅkhāras*, for the relinquishment of all acquisitions, for the ending of *taṇhā*, for dispassion, cessation, and *nibbāna*.
mn22:21.6: It does not occur to him:
mn22:21.7: ‘So I shall be annihilated! So I shall perish! So I shall no longer exist!’
mn22:21.8: He does not sorrow, does not wear himself out, does not lament, does not beat his breast and weep, and does not fall into confusion.
mn22:21.9: That is how, *bhikkhu*, there is non-distress over what is internal when it is absent.
mn22:22.1: Bhikkhus, would you take up any possession such that that possession, once possessed, would be permanent, stable, everlasting, not liable to change, and would remain just so for eternity?
mn22:22.2: Do you see, bhikkhus, any such possession, such that that possession, once possessed, would be permanent, stable, everlasting, not liable to change, and would remain just so for eternity?”
mn22:22.5: I too, bhikkhus, do not see any such possession, such that that possession, once possessed, would be permanent, stable, everlasting, not liable to change, and would remain just so for eternity.
mn22:23.1: Bhikkhus, would you take up any clinging/feeding [*attavādupādānaṁ*] to a doctrine of self such that, when taking up that clinging/feeding [*attavādupādānaṁ*], sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and distress would not arise?
mn22:23.2: Do you see, bhikkhus, any such clinging/feeding [*attavādupādānaṁ*] to a doctrine of self such that, when taking it up, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and distress would not arise?”
mn22:23.5: I too, bhikkhus, do not see any such clinging/feeding [*attavādupādānaṁ*] to a doctrine of self such that, when taking it up, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and distress would not arise.
mn22:24.1: Bhikkhus, would you rely on any support of view such that, when relying on that support of view, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and distress would not arise?
mn22:24.2: Do you see, bhikkhus, any such support of view such that, when relying on it, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and distress would not arise?”
mn22:24.5: I too, bhikkhus, do not see any such support of view such that, when relying on it, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and distress would not arise.
mn22:25.1: “Bhikkhus, if there were a self, would there be for me what belongs to self?”
mn22:25.3: “Or if there were what belongs to self, would there be for me a self?”
mn22:25.5: “But when self and what belongs to self are not apprehended truly and actually, then whatever that view-ground is:
mn22:25.6: ‘That is the world, that is the self. After death I shall be permanent, stable, everlasting, not liable to change; I shall remain just so for eternity’—
mn22:25.7: is this not, bhikkhus, a sheer and complete fool’s *dhamma*?”
mn22:25.8: “What else could it be, bhante? It is indeed, bhante, a sheer and complete fool’s *dhamma*.”
mn22:26.15: “And what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], is that *dukkha* or *sukha*?”
mn22:26.17: “And what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], *dukkha*, and subject to change, is it fitting to regard that thus—
mn22:26.18: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?”
mn22:26.4: “And what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], is that *dukkha* or *sukha*?”
mn22:26.6: “And what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], *dukkha*, and subject to change, is it fitting to regard that thus—
mn22:26.7: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?”
mn22:27.1: “Therefore, bhikkhus, whatever *rūpa* there is—past, future, or present, internal or external, coarse or subtle, inferior or superior, far or near—all *rūpa* should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*] thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn22:27.5: whatever consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*] there is—past, future, or present, internal or external, coarse or subtle, inferior or superior, far or near—all consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*] should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*] thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn22:28.1: Seeing thus, bhikkhus, the instructed noble disciple becomes disenchanted with *rūpa*, becomes disenchanted with *vedanā*, becomes disenchanted with *saññā*, becomes disenchanted with *saṅkhāras*, becomes disenchanted with consciousness [*viññāṇasmiṁ*],
mn22:29.1: Through disenchantment he becomes dispassionate; through dispassion he is liberated. When liberated, there is the knowledge: ‘Liberated.’
mn22:29.2: He understands: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’
mn22:3.1: Then those *bhikkhus* went to Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter. Having gone to him, they said this to him:
mn22:3.10: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as like a piece of meat …
mn22:3.11: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as like a grass torch …
mn22:3.12: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as like a pit of glowing coals …
mn22:3.13: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as like a dream …
mn22:3.14: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as like borrowed goods …
mn22:3.15: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as like fruits on a tree …
mn22:3.16: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as like a butcher’s cleaver and block …
mn22:3.17: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as like swords and spears …
mn22:3.18: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as like a snake’s head, with much *dukkha*, much distress, and greater danger in them.”
mn22:3.19: Yet even when Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter, was being questioned, pressed, and admonished by those *bhikkhus*, he kept insisting on that same bad view, holding to it stubbornly and clinging to it:
mn22:3.2: “Is it really true, friend Ariṭṭha, that this bad view has arisen in you:
mn22:3.20: “That is exactly how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the Bhagavā, friends: those obstructive *dhammas* spoken of by the Bhagavā are not capable of obstructing one who engages in them.”
mn22:3.21: When those *bhikkhus* were unable to detach Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter, from that bad view, they went to the Bhagavā. Having gone to the Bhagavā, they bowed to him and sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, those *bhikkhus* said this to the Bhagavā:
mn22:3.22: “Bhante, a bad view has arisen in the *bhikkhu* named Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter:
mn22:3.23: ‘I understand the *dhamma* taught by the Bhagavā like this: those obstructive *dhammas* spoken of by the Bhagavā are not capable of obstructing one who engages in them.’
mn22:3.25: ‘It seems that a bad view has arisen in the *bhikkhu* named Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter—
mn22:3.26: I understand the *dhamma* taught by the Bhagavā like this: those obstructive *dhammas* spoken of by the Bhagavā are not capable of obstructing one who engages in them.’
mn22:3.27: Then we went to Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter. Having gone to him, we said this to him:
mn22:3.28: ‘Is it really true, friend Ariṭṭha, that this bad view has arisen in you—
mn22:3.29: I understand the *dhamma* taught by the Bhagavā like this: those obstructive *dhammas* spoken of by the Bhagavā are not capable of obstructing one who engages in them?’
mn22:3.3: ‘I understand the *dhamma* taught by the Bhagavā like this: those obstructive *dhammas* spoken of by the Bhagavā are not capable of obstructing one who engages in them’?”
mn22:3.30: When this was said, bhante, Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter, said this to us:
mn22:3.31: ‘That is exactly how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the Bhagavā, friends: those obstructive *dhammas* spoken of by the Bhagavā are not capable of obstructing one who engages in them.’
mn22:3.32: Then, bhante, wanting to detach Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter, from that bad view, we questioned him, pressed him, and admonished him:
mn22:3.33: ‘Do not say so, friend Ariṭṭha. Do not misrepresent the Bhagavā; it is not good to misrepresent the Bhagavā. The Bhagavā would not say that.
mn22:3.34: In many ways, friend Ariṭṭha, obstructive *dhammas* have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as obstructive, and they are indeed capable of obstructing one who engages in them.
mn22:3.35: Sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as having little gratification, much *dukkha*, much distress, and greater danger in them.
mn22:3.36: Sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as like a skeleton …
mn22:3.37: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as like a snake’s head, with much *dukkha*, much distress, and greater danger in them.’
mn22:3.38: Yet even when Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter, was being questioned, pressed, and admonished by us, he kept insisting on that same bad view, holding to it stubbornly and clinging to it:
mn22:3.39: ‘That is exactly how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the Bhagavā, friends: those obstructive *dhammas* spoken of by the Bhagavā are not capable of obstructing one who engages in them.’
mn22:3.4: “That is exactly how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the Bhagavā, friends: those obstructive *dhammas* spoken of by the Bhagavā are not capable of obstructing one who engages in them.”
mn22:3.5: Then those *bhikkhus*, wanting to detach Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter, from that bad view, questioned him, pressed him, and admonished him:
mn22:3.6: “Do not say so, friend Ariṭṭha. Do not misrepresent the Bhagavā; it is not good to misrepresent the Bhagavā. The Bhagavā would not say that.
mn22:3.7: In many ways, friend Ariṭṭha, obstructive *dhammas* have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as obstructive, and they are indeed capable of obstructing one who engages in them.
mn22:3.8: Sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as having little gratification, much *dukkha*, much distress, and greater danger in them.
mn22:3.9: Sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as like a skeleton …
mn22:30.1: Bhikkhus, this is called a *bhikkhu* with the crossbar lifted, with the moat filled in, with the pillar uprooted, without bolt, a noble one with banner lowered, burden laid down, detached.
mn22:31.1: And how, bhikkhus, is a *bhikkhu* one with the crossbar lifted?
mn22:31.2: Here, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu’s* blindness [*avijjā*] has been abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, brought to non-being, not liable to arise in the future.
mn22:31.3: That is how, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* is one with the crossbar lifted.
mn22:32.1: And how, bhikkhus, is a *bhikkhu* one with the moat filled in?
mn22:32.2: Here, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu’s* wandering-on in birth and *saṃsāra* leading to renewed existence has been abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, brought to non-being, not liable to arise in the future.
mn22:32.3: That is how, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* is one with the moat filled in.
mn22:33.1: And how, bhikkhus, is a *bhikkhu* one with the pillar uprooted?
mn22:33.2: Here, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu’s* *taṇhā* has been abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, brought to non-being, not liable to arise in the future.
mn22:33.3: That is how, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* is one with the pillar uprooted.
mn22:34.1: And how, bhikkhus, is a *bhikkhu* without bolt?
mn22:34.2: Here, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu’s* five lower fetters have been abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, brought to non-being, not liable to arise in the future.
mn22:34.3: That is how, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* is without bolt.
mn22:35.1: And how, bhikkhus, is a *bhikkhu* a noble one with banner lowered, burden laid down, detached?
mn22:35.2: Here, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu’s* conceit ‘I am’ has been abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, brought to non-being, not liable to arise in the future.
mn22:35.3: That is how, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* is a noble one with banner lowered, burden laid down, detached.
mn22:36.1: Bhikkhus, when a *bhikkhu* has a mind thus liberated [*vimuttacittaṁ*], devas together with Inda, with Brahmā, and with Pajāpati, searching, do not find:
mn22:36.2: ‘The consciousness [*viññāṇan*] of the Tathāgata depends on this.’
mn22:36.4: Because, bhikkhus, I say that even here and now the Tathāgata is not traceable.
mn22:37.1: Yet because I speak thus and explain thus, some ascetics and brahmins misrepresent me falsely, emptily, vainly, untruly, with what is not the case:
mn22:37.2: ‘The ascetic Gotama is one who teaches elimination; he teaches the annihilation, destruction, and extermination of an existing being.’
mn22:37.3: As I am not, bhikkhus, and as I do not say, so those worthy ascetics and brahmins misrepresent me falsely, emptily, vainly, untruly, with what is not the case:
mn22:37.4: ‘The ascetic Gotama is one who teaches elimination; he teaches the annihilation, destruction, and extermination of an existing being.’
mn22:37.5: Formerly and now as well, bhikkhus, I make known only *dukkha* and the cessation of *dukkha*.
mn22:37.6: If others abuse, revile, provoke, and harass the Tathāgata because of that, then, bhikkhus, no resentment, displeasure, or discontent of mind [*cetaso*] arises in the Tathāgata.
mn22:38.1: And if others honor, respect, esteem, and venerate the Tathāgata because of that, then, bhikkhus, no joy, gladness, or exhilaration of mind [*cetaso*] arises in the Tathāgata.
mn22:38.2: And if others honor, respect, esteem, and venerate the Tathāgata because of that, then, bhikkhus, this occurs to the Tathāgata:
mn22:38.3: ‘This is done toward what has already been fully understood by me.’
mn22:39.1: Therefore, bhikkhus, if others were to abuse, revile, provoke, and harass you, then no resentment, displeasure, or discontent of mind [*cetaso*] should be produced by you there.
mn22:39.2: Therefore, bhikkhus, if others were to honor, respect, esteem, and venerate you, then no joy, gladness, or exhilaration of mind [*cetaso*] should be produced by you there.
mn22:39.3: Therefore, bhikkhus, if others were to honor, respect, esteem, and venerate you, then this should occur to you:
mn22:39.4: ‘This is done toward what has already been fully understood by me.’
mn22:4.1: Since, bhante, we were unable to detach Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter, from that bad view, we have reported this matter to the Bhagavā.”
mn22:40.1: Therefore, bhikkhus, abandon what is not yours.
mn22:40.2: When abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.1: And what, bhikkhus, is not yours?
mn22:41.10: Consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*], bhikkhus, is not yours. Abandon it.
mn22:41.11: When abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.13: if people were to carry off, burn, or do as they pleased with the grass, sticks, branches, and leaves in this Jeta’s Grove,
mn22:41.15: ‘People are carrying us off, burning us, or doing as they please with us’?”
mn22:41.18: “Because, bhante, that is not our self or what belongs to our self.”
mn22:41.19: “In the same way, bhikkhus, abandon what is not yours.
mn22:41.2: *Rūpa*, bhikkhus, is not yours. Abandon it.
mn22:41.20: When abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.21: And what, bhikkhus, is not yours?
mn22:41.22: *Rūpa*, bhikkhus, is not yours. Abandon it.
mn22:41.23: When abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.27: consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*], bhikkhus, is not yours. Abandon it.
mn22:41.28: When abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.3: When abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.4: *Vedanā*, bhikkhus, is not yours. Abandon it.
mn22:41.5: When abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.6: *Saññā*, bhikkhus, is not yours. Abandon it.
mn22:41.7: When abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.8: *Saṅkhāras*, bhikkhus, are not yours. Abandon them.
mn22:41.9: When abandoned by you, they will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:42.1: Thus, bhikkhus, the *dhamma* has been well taught by me—plain, open, clear, revealed, with the patchwork cut away.
mn22:42.2: Thus, bhikkhus, when the *dhamma* has been well taught by me—plain, open, clear, revealed, with the patchwork cut away—those *bhikkhus* who are *arahants*, with *āsavas* destroyed [*khīṇāsavā*], who have lived the holy life, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, reached their own goal, completely destroyed the fetters of *bhava*, fully liberated through complete knowing, for them there is no round left to be described.
mn22:43.1: Thus, bhikkhus, the *dhamma* has been well taught by me—plain, open, clear, revealed, with the patchwork cut away.
mn22:43.2: Thus, bhikkhus, when the *dhamma* has been well taught by me—plain, open, clear, revealed, with the patchwork cut away—those *bhikkhus* whose five lower fetters have been abandoned, all of them are spontaneously reborn, attaining final *nibbāna* there, not liable to return from that world.
mn22:44.1: Thus, bhikkhus, the *dhamma* has been well taught by me—plain, open, clear, revealed, with the patchwork cut away.
mn22:44.2: Thus, bhikkhus, when the *dhamma* has been well taught by me—plain, open, clear, revealed, with the patchwork cut away—those *bhikkhus* whose three fetters have been abandoned, and whose lust, aversion, and delusion have been thinned out, all of them are once-returners; returning to this world just once more, they will make an end of *dukkha*.
mn22:45.1: Thus, bhikkhus, the *dhamma* has been well taught by me—plain, open, clear, revealed, with the patchwork cut away.
mn22:45.2: Thus, bhikkhus, when the *dhamma* has been well taught by me—plain, open, clear, revealed, with the patchwork cut away—those *bhikkhus* whose three fetters have been abandoned, all of them are stream-enterers, not liable to downfall, fixed in destiny, headed for full awakening.
mn22:46.1: Thus, bhikkhus, the *dhamma* has been well taught by me—plain, open, clear, revealed, with the patchwork cut away.
mn22:46.2: Thus, bhikkhus, when the *dhamma* has been well taught by me—plain, open, clear, revealed, with the patchwork cut away—those *bhikkhus* who are followers of the *dhamma* and followers by faith, all of them are headed for full awakening.
mn22:47.1: Thus, bhikkhus, the *dhamma* has been well taught by me—plain, open, clear, revealed, with the patchwork cut away.
mn22:47.2: Thus, bhikkhus, when the *dhamma* has been well taught by me—plain, open, clear, revealed, with the patchwork cut away—those who have only a measure of faith in me, only a measure of love for me, all of them are headed for heaven.”
mn22:47.3: This is what the Bhagavā said.
mn22:47.4: Delighted, those *bhikkhus* rejoiced in what the Bhagavā had said.
mn22:47.5: The Simile of the Snake, the second, is finished.
mn22:5.1: Then the Bhagavā addressed a certain *bhikkhu*:
mn22:5.2: “Come, *bhikkhu*, in my name address Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter:
mn22:5.3: ‘The Teacher is calling you, friend Ariṭṭha.’”
mn22:5.4: “Yes, bhante,” that *bhikkhu* replied to the Bhagavā. Then he went to Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter, and said to him:
mn22:5.5: “The Teacher is calling you, friend Ariṭṭha.”
mn22:5.6: “Yes, friend,” Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter, replied to that *bhikkhu*. Then he went to the Bhagavā. Having gone to the Bhagavā, he bowed to him and sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter, was addressed by the Bhagavā:
mn22:5.7: “Is it really true, Ariṭṭha, that this bad view has arisen in you:
mn22:5.8: ‘I understand the *dhamma* taught by the Bhagavā like this: those obstructive *dhammas* spoken of by the Bhagavā are not capable of obstructing one who engages in them’?”
mn22:5.9: “That is exactly how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the Bhagavā, bhante: ‘those obstructive *dhammas* spoken of by the Bhagavā are not capable of obstructing one who engages in them.’”
mn22:6.1: “By whom, foolish man, have you understood me to teach the *dhamma* in that way?
mn22:6.10: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by me as like fruits on a tree …
mn22:6.11: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by me as like a butcher’s cleaver and block …
mn22:6.12: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by me as like swords and spears …
mn22:6.13: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by me as like a snake’s head, with much *dukkha*, much distress, and greater danger in them.
mn22:6.14: And yet you, foolish man, through your own wrong grasp, both misrepresent us and dig yourself into a pit, and you generate much demerit.
mn22:6.15: For that will lead to your harm and *dukkha* for a long time, foolish man.”
mn22:6.2: Have I not, foolish man, in many ways spoken of obstructive *dhammas* as obstructive? And are they not indeed capable of obstructing one who engages in them?
mn22:6.3: Sensual pleasures have been spoken of by me as having little gratification, much *dukkha*, much distress, and greater danger in them.
mn22:6.4: Sensual pleasures have been spoken of by me as like a skeleton …
mn22:6.5: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by me as like a piece of meat …
mn22:6.6: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by me as like a grass torch …
mn22:6.7: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by me as like a pit of glowing coals …
mn22:6.8: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by me as like a dream …
mn22:6.9: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by me as like borrowed goods …
mn22:7.3: is this Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter, even slightly warmed in this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*]?”
mn22:7.6: When this was said, Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter, sat silent, ashamed, shoulders drooping, head down, brooding, unable to reply.
mn22:7.7: Then the Bhagavā, knowing that Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter, was silent, ashamed, shoulders drooping, head down, brooding, and unable to reply, said this to him:
mn22:7.8: “You will become known, foolish man, by this own bad view of yours.
mn22:7.9: Now I shall question the *bhikkhus* about this.”
mn22:8.10: For in many ways, bhikkhus, obstructive *dhammas* have been spoken of by me, and they are indeed capable of obstructing one who engages in them.
mn22:8.11: Sensual pleasures have been spoken of by me as having little gratification, much *dukkha*, much distress, and greater danger in them.
mn22:8.12: Sensual pleasures have been spoken of by me as like a skeleton …
mn22:8.13: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by me as like a snake’s head, with much *dukkha*, much distress, and greater danger in them.
mn22:8.14: And yet this Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter, through his own wrong grasp, both misrepresents us and digs himself into a pit and generates much demerit.
mn22:8.15: For that will lead to the harm and *dukkha* of that foolish man for a long time.
mn22:8.2: “Do you too understand me to teach the *dhamma* in the way that this Ariṭṭha the *bhikkhu*, formerly a vulture-hunter, through his own wrong grasp, both misrepresents us and digs himself into a pit and generates much demerit?”
mn22:8.4: For in many ways, bhante, obstructive *dhammas* have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as obstructive;
mn22:8.5: and they are indeed capable of obstructing one who engages in them.
mn22:8.6: Sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as having little gratification, much *dukkha*, much distress, and greater danger in them.
mn22:8.7: Sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as like a skeleton …
mn22:8.8: sensual pleasures have been spoken of by the Bhagavā as like a snake’s head, with much *dukkha*, much distress, and greater danger in them.”
mn22:8.9: “Sādhu, sādhu, bhikkhus. It is good, bhikkhus, that you understand me to teach the *dhamma* in this way.
mn22:9.1: Bhikkhus, that one should engage in sensual pleasures apart from sensual pleasures, apart from the *saññā* of sensual pleasures, apart from thoughts [*vitakkā*] of sensual pleasures—this is impossible.
